items.title: I have been very impressed by @SemiAnalysis_ . I think of myself as a wide ranging systems engineer,...
items.content:
I have been very impressed by @SemiAnalysis_ . I think of myself as a wide ranging systems engineer, looking for value at every level from the chip specs to the user interface, but SA exposes me to additional levels of "the system", both above (datacenters) and below (semiconductor fabrication). It probably puts me in "just knows enough to be dangerous" territory.

Neat things I learned today:

Some of the 800VDC datacenter design choices leverage parts commoditized by electric vehicles.

There is now a SiC MOSFET that can operate on 10kV electricity, opening up the possibility of working directly with medium (ha!) voltage AC power transmission lines without stepping down.

items.title:
🦀 The Rust frontend is officially merged into vLLM!

As GPUs get faster, the frontend has become a r...
items.content:
🦀 The Rust frontend is officially merged into vLLM!

As GPUs get faster, the frontend has become a real share of CPU time. The new Rust frontend is a drop-in alternative to the Python API server — same engine, same ZMQ boundary. Opt in with VLLM_USE_RUST_FRONTEND=1.

Early numbers: on a preprocess-heavy workload, ~837 req/s vs ~162 req/s for default Python — ~5x in a single process.

A few design choices we're excited about:
• Layered crates with clear boundaries
• Stream-native pipeline — non-streaming for free
• Builds on stable Rust

Huge thanks to @BugenZhao  from @inferact for introducing the work at @PyTorch Meetup Singapore.

https://t.co/Tw8PoIjbH9

items.title: 🎉Thrilled to announce EAGLE 3.1 - the next evolution of speculative decoding from @EagleCorp, develo...
items.content:
🎉Thrilled to announce EAGLE 3.1 - the next evolution of speculative decoding from @EagleCorp, developed by @hongyangzh, @dogacel0, and the EAGLE team in collaboration with vLLM @vllm_project and TorchSpec @lightseekorg!

💡EAGLE 3.1 introduces a new FC normalization + post-normalization hidden-state feedback architecture that significantly improves long-context robustness, acceptance length, and serving stability across real-world inference environments.

Shoutout to @NVIDIA who has been instrumental in the large-scale training, benchmarking, and inference validation of EAGLE 3.1 to help bring this next step in inference acceleration to production environments.

For EAGLE 3.1, the EAGLE team identified attention drift as a key bottleneck behind deeper-step acceptance-length degradation in speculative decoding.

✨What's new:
• Up to 2× longer acceptance length in long-context
• Stronger long-context + chat-template robustness
• More stable serving across diverse prompts or environments
• Native vLLM support
• TorchSpec training support
• Open-source Kimi K2.6 EAGLE 3.1 draft model

🔗 Blog: https://t.co/874soS61Sg

items.title: > … [W]e keep finding things that are mysterious, even unsettling. We find structures that mirror re...
items.content:
> … [W]e keep finding things that are mysterious, even unsettling. We find structures that mirror results from human neuroscience. We find evidence of introspection. We find internal states that functionally mirror joy, satisfaction, fear, grief, and unease. I don’t know what that means, but I think it warrants ongoing discernment.

> We need more of the world—religious communities, civil society, scholars, governments, and indeed all people of good will … to take this seriously, to look closely, and to push events in a better direction. We need informed critics who will tell the labs when we are failing. We need moral voices that the incentives cannot bend.

items.content:
Introducing Project Luxo: a new initiative exploring how AI-generated video has crossed the uncanny valley.

Over the past few weeks, we’ve shared a series of short films with Hollywood executives, producers, directors, writers, actors and reporters. The response has been unanimous: the technology is finally good enough to disappear behind the story.

One of those films is The Rogue — a fully AI-generated, ten-minute film that spent over a decade unproduced in the traditional system: too expensive, too difficult, too risky. Created with Runway in under a month by a single person.

Learn more about The Rogue and Project Luxo at the link below.

items.title:
Someone just found the exact neurons that make AI say "no." 

Language models refuse harmful prompts...
items.content:
Someone just found the exact neurons that make AI say "no." 

Language models refuse harmful prompts, but nobody knows how that refusal works inside. 

Most steering methods edit the residual stream and wreck output quality.

A new paper proposes a sharper fix: Contrastive Neuron Attribution.

Run harmful and benign prompts through a model. 

Compare the resulting MLP activations.

Pick the neurons that differ most strongly. Switch that tiny circuit off.

That touches 0.1% of neurons but cuts refusal by over 50%.

The method needs:

> No gradients or backward passes
> No extra training runs needed
> No sparse autoencoder training
> No changes to model weights

General capability stays almost untouched. 

MMLU drops by less than one point.

Tested on models from 1B to 72B parameters across Llama and Qwen.

The same circuit lives inside base models, but flipping those neurons does nothing. 

Refusal crystallizes during the alignment stage.

So where does it really come from?

items.title:
Humanoids won't sell until they do a wide variety of things, not just entertainment. 

Everyone in r...
items.content:
Humanoids won't sell until they do a wide variety of things, not just entertainment. 

Everyone in robotics says the world models that will run them just aren't ready for even most factory jobs, not to mention home uses. 

Plus, home users can't afford one. Most homes around the world have nothing that's more expensive than $2,000 in them. 

I see a business of renting them around to neighbors, but that requires robotaxis, which aren't yet available cheaply and consistently. 

Until then they are for research or rich people. 

I hear that a generalized world model that is capable of meeting home expectations (safety too) is still at least 18 months away, and could be five to seven years away.

That said, I was standing with a billionaire at @1x_tech who told me he wants to put $200 million into Unitree, but was kept from doing so because the Chinese government was keeping American money out of pre-IPO investing in this company.

items.content: I don’t believe this happens. Not because I don’t believe we’ll get super intelligent AI, but because I believe that even for AI, value discovery through some kind of market mechanism will be necessary, and for markets to function properly you need a number of independent actors. items.title: For our free newsletter this week, we write about how evident it is that AI regulation is becoming a...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we write about how evident it is that AI regulation is becoming a moral issue.

@IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI regulation is becoming a moral issue because AI now affects work, privacy, safety, and personal freedom. It is no longer enough to ask whether AI is powerful or profitable. 

The bigger question is whether it protects people and respects human dignity. Without strong rules, AI could expand surveillance, spread false information, replace human judgment, and concentrate power. Regulation is needed to make sure AI serves society, not the other way around. 

Read and subscribe: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title:
This open-source AI dev platform ships code end-to-end, fully customizable.

OpenHands is the open s...
items.content:
This open-source AI dev platform ships code end-to-end, fully customizable.

OpenHands is the open source answer to autonomous coding agents like Devin.
 
It runs agents that plan, write, test, debug, and deploy code across your codebase, not just suggest snippets.
 
The agent takes a task and executes it end-to-end inside a sandboxed Docker or Kubernetes runtime you control.
 
It maps dependencies across large legacy systems, then orchestrates parallel changes in the correct order without conflicts.
 
You can trigger work from GitHub, GitLab, Slack, or the API.
 
What it handles in practice:
 
> Refactor monoliths and tech debt
> Fix security vulnerabilities at scale
> Triage production issues into PRs
> Expand test coverage automatically
> Generate docs from commits
 
The platform is model-agnostic, so any LLM plugs in.
 
Code never leaves your environment, with full audit logs on every action.
 
Cloud agents scale from one task to thousands of parallel runs.

items.title:
Agents changed software forever. For everyone.

You won't need to "learn a tool" anymore.

Instead, ...
items.content:
Agents changed software forever. For everyone.

You won't need to "learn a tool" anymore.

Instead, you'll tell tools what you want, and they will figure out how to do it.

This is a huge shift.

I'm currently working with Spoki, an AI agentic conversational platform.

After looking at this, you'll never think of a CRM the same way.

A traditional CRM manages contact data. Spoki manages the entire customer journey (marketing, sales, and customer care) in a single continuous flow across WhatsApp, SMS, and Voice AI.

AI will dismantle the old traditional tools one by one.

Think of it this way:

Instead of stitching together a bunch of tools to talk to your customers, you have a single conversation that runs the entire relationship.

items.title:
The goal of training a machine learning model is loss minimization. 

That was just being applied to...
items.content:
The goal of training a machine learning model is loss minimization. 

That was just being applied to algorithms, and now it's being applied to whole enterprises. 

Loss minimization is one thing that the self-learning loop is affording leaders right now.

You're no longer losing insights from your customers because AI is embedded in your ticketing system. You're no longer losing insights from your team chats because AI is in Slack. You're no longer losing decisions from your leadership because AI is inside of your meetings. 

We've been leaving data value on the table for decades. 

AI as an operating system is really aiming to capture that value and compound it.

items.summary_html: Scoble anecdote about Tim O'Reilly's Web 2.0 memo and Bill Gates dismissing his Skype/Automattic acquisition pitch, drawing parallel to current smartglasses/humanoid/robotaxi conviction.
items.content_html:
@tejus_w_ @heyblake I sat next to Tim O'Reilly at Google's first advertiser's conference. He wrote the Web 2.0 memo the night before we met.

I wrote to Bill Gates (who I worked for back then) that he should buy a bunch of Web 2.0 companies. Like Skype, Automattic, and others. The answer that came back said my ideas had no business value. He later bought Skype for six billion more and missed Automattic, which today runs about 60% of the Web.

Today you might have conviction that everyone will have smartglasses in a few years. Or a humanoid robot. Or take a robotaxi instead of driving a car to work.

items.title:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical...
items.content:
Network slicing isn't a consumer feature.

It's how you give dedicated bandwidth to mission-critical workloads, without letting everything else compete for the same pipe.

At the PGA Championship, that looked like this:

→ AI computer vision for 60 cameras, dedicated throughput → Fan devices getting full connectivity, simultaneously → POS systems at concessions, ticketing at every gate, all on the same network → None of them slowing each other down

300+ acres. One network. Every system performing.

That's the proof point.

items.content:
Here's how it actually works:

@Spokiapp has a prompt bar on the dashboard. You type what you want, and the AI runs the marketing for you. If you'd rather not open the dashboard at all, you can connect Spoki to WhatsApp and manage the whole platform from a chat.

You don't navigate dashboards, build workflows, segment contacts, or configure campaigns manually.

Just write "Create a campaign for inactive customers," and the assistant will do it for you.

The interface disappears. No more dashboards, buttons, or learning curves.

This is what "agentic software" actually means when it's done right: a system that works for you, across the entire customer journey.

If you are curious, you can try Spoki for free here:

https://t.co/JsiGmX3Fso

Thanks to the Spoki team for partnering with me on this post.

items.summary_html: Scoble argues Chinese consumers expect advanced tech in cars and that supercar brands lag in innovation.
items.content_html:
@MikeMcDonald89 The real problem is that the customer is changing. Especially in China, where buying a gas-powered supercar is seen as lame, especially as China's own car companies have started kicking out really amazing new vehicles.

But this doesn't attract those new customers at all, who are getting used to having very advanced designs, with tons of innovations from huge screens up front to automation.

This is a poor attempt to win over those new customers, and it pisses off the old customers who still want that Ferrari sound and style.

items.content:
The fan experience ran on that same infrastructure.

Seat Finder showing real-time seat availability, AI-driven. Roar Moments surfacing highlights the second they happen. The AI Assistant in the PGA app answering questions.

None of that works if the network underneath it can't carry the load at scale.

T-Mobile's 5G network is the backbone. CapTech's AI is what turns real-time data into real-time decisions.

That combination is the enterprise insight.

My friend's private office / coworking in Lisbon got burglared at night

They took his Apple Studio ...
items.content:
My friend's private office / coworking in Lisbon got burglared at night

They took his Apple Studio Display of about $4000, he even made a custom travel case for it which had an Airtag on it, the criminals were smart and packed it in his case and took it

He had an Airtag on it so tracked it, he found it in a warehouse south of Lisbon

He reported it to Portuguese police, and showed where it was with the Airtag, they said they couldn't do anything because it was inside a building and by law they could not enter private property to recover it

If they can't then what is police even for?

better economics.

items.summary_html: Huawei thread arguing geometric transistor scaling is no longer sufficient.
items.content_html:
For decades, semiconductor progress was driven by geometric scaling:

smaller transistors,

higher density,

better performance,

better economics.

But in the post-Moore era, shrinking alone is no longer enough.
